lines.text: Section seven. Modelling — turning biology into equations.
Up to this point, the chapter has been about the discipline of engineering biology — frameworks, hierarchies, registries, design tools.
Now we get to the part where engineering bites: the equations.
Synthetic biology, if it is to live up to its predictive-design ambitions, needs mathematical models of biological devices that are accurate enough to guide design decisions.
The authors illustrate this with a simple but fundamental example: a transcriptional regulatory device.
Imagine a small piece of DNA that contains an inducible promoter, a ribosome binding site, a protein-coding region, and a terminator.
In the presence of a transcription factor, the promoter activates, the gene is transcribed into messenger RNA, and the messenger RNA is then translated into protein.
How can we describe this dynamically — in equations — so a computer can simulate it?
The standard approach is to use ordinary differential equations, abbreviated ODEs, that track the concentrations of mRNA and protein over time.
Two equations capture the system.
The first equation describes the rate of change of mRNA concentration over time.
The mRNA is produced at a rate that depends on the concentration of the transcription factor, and it is degraded at a rate proportional to how much mRNA is currently present.

Mathematically, the production term takes a particular nonlinear form called a Hill function, parameterised by the transcription factor concentration W, a binding constant K, and a Hill coefficient n which captures cooperativity — exactly the cooperativity we talked about in chapter one's discussion of transcription factors.
If the transcription factor is an activator, a parameter mu equals plus one and the mRNA production scales up with W.
If the transcription factor is a repressor, mu equals zero and the relationship inverts.
Multiplying this Hill term by a kinetic rate constant of transcription, ktr, gives you the production rate.
Subtracting a degradation term — a degradation rate constant dm multiplied by the current mRNA concentration — gives you the net rate of change.
So equation one is, in words: rate of change of mRNA equals Hill-shaped production rate driven by the transcription factor minus first-order degradation.
The second equation handles the next step in the central dogma: translation.
The protein P is produced at a rate proportional to mRNA concentration, with the proportionality being a kinetic rate constant of translation, ktl.
And the protein is itself degraded at a rate proportional to its concentration, with a degradation rate constant dp.
So equation two is, in words: rate of change of protein equals translation rate from mRNA minus first-order protein degradation.
This pair of equations is genuinely usable.
Plug in numerical values for ktr, K, n, dm, ktl, and dp, simulate the system over time on a laptop, and you have a quantitative prediction of how your device will behave.

And these same two equations, with small modifications to the Hill function, can describe a whole family of related devices — inverters, switches, simple genetic circuits — by varying the parameters and the sign of the regulation.
Here is the fundamental challenge, though, and the authors are honest about it.
As you scale up — combining many devices into a larger system — the ODE description gets large fast.
You have an mRNA and a protein for every gene; you have feedback loops connecting them; you have shared resources like ribosomes and polymerases that compete across genes.
At some point the differential-equations description, while still in principle correct, stops being something a human can reason about by inspection.
You can simulate it, you can run parameter sweeps, but you lose the intuitive feel that lets a designer say "if I increase ktl on this gene, the steady-state protein level rises about this much."
Engineering has faced this kind of problem many times before.
The mathematical machinery that engineers reach for in this situation is called systems and control theory.
Systems and control theory was developed in the early nineteenth century by, primarily, two French mathematicians: Pierre-Simon Laplace and Joseph Fourier.
Laplace's contribution is the Laplace transform, a mathematical operation that converts an ordinary differential equation into an algebraic equation in a new variable called s.
In the Laplace domain, the differential equation becomes much easier to manipulate — composition, feedback, and parallel structures all become straightforward algebra.
Once you have solved the problem in the Laplace domain, you transform back to the time domain — the real world — and recover the behaviour as a function of time.

Fourier's contribution, complementary to Laplace's, is the Fourier transform, which decomposes any signal into its frequency components, opening up the entire frequency-domain perspective on dynamical systems.
Both of these are foundational tools that we will get to in the next two sections.

The reason they matter for synthetic biology is exactly the reason they matter for any other engineering discipline: they let you reason about complex systems without drowning in the underlying differential equations.
And synthetic biology's complex systems are going to be the rule, not the exception.
